content:
6 min read
Just now

--

I extend the Product model with category labels and display them on every card without rebuilding the catalog layout.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Episode 17 begins with a catalog that is cleaner underneath, but still a little quiet on the screen.
The product data now lives inside one organized list, and Flutter builds every card automatically. That was an important step, but all three products still look almost identical.
A pair of headphones, a smart watch, and a digital camera should not feel like they came from the same anonymous cardboard box.
So in this episode, I will give every product a category and display that category directly on the card.
I will extend the `Product` model, add category information to the product list, and update the card layout so the new labels are easy to notice.
I will also improve the spacing inside each card and make the product information feel more organized.
The catalog will still use the same reusable structure from Episode 16, but the result on the screen should feel more informative and more alive.
The stockroom is organized.
Now it is time to put labels on the shelves.
Before adding category labels, I first locate the three parts of `main.dart` that need to change:
- the `Product` model,
- the product list,
- and the card builder.
The structure from Episode 16 is already working, so I do not need to rebuild anything. I only need to extend it carefully.
A good refactor should grow like a bookshelf, not collapse like furniture assembled without the manual.
cd ~/offline_product_catalog
grep -nE "class Product|const products|products\.map" lib/main.dartPress enter or click to view image in full size

The terminal locates the product model, product list, and reusable card builder that will be updated with category information.The three important sections are now identified, so I can update the data and the interface together.
First, I will extend the `Product` model with a category field.
Then I will give each product its own category:
Wireless Headphones belongs to Audio.
Smart Watch belongs to Wearables.
Digital Camera belongs to Photography.
I will also redesign the cards with clearer spacing, a category label, a dedicated icon box, and a stronger price style.
The reusable catalog structure will stay intact. It is simply getting better clothes.
cp lib/main.dart lib/main.dart.backup_episode17python3 - <<'PY'
from pathlib import Path
file_path = Path("lib/main.dart")
text = file_path.read_text()
old_constructor = """ const Product({
required this.name,
required this.description,
required this.price,
required this.icon,
});"""
new_constructor = """ const Product({
required this.name,
required this.description,
required this.price,
required this.icon,
required this.category,
});"""
old_fields = """ final String price;
final IconData icon;"""
new_fields = """ final String price;
final IconData icon;
final String category;"""
product_updates = {
""" icon: Icons.headphones,
),""": """ icon: Icons.headphones,
category: 'Audio',
),""",
""" icon: Icons.watch,
),""": """ icon: Icons.watch,
category: 'Wearables',
),""",
""" icon: Icons.camera_alt,
),""": """ icon: Icons.camera_alt,
category: 'Photography',
),""",
}
old_builder = """ ...products.map(
(product) => Padding(
padding: const EdgeInsets.only(bottom: 12),
child: Card(
child: ListTile(
leading: Icon(product.icon),
title: Text(product.name),
subtitle: Text(product.description),
trailing: Text(product.price),
),
),
),
),"""
new_builder = """ ...products.map(
(product) => Padding(
padding: const EdgeInsets.only(bottom: 16),
child: Card(
elevation: 1,
shape: RoundedRectangleBorder(
borderRadius: BorderRadius.circular(16),
),
child: Padding(
padding: const EdgeInsets.all(16),
child: Row(
crossAxisAlignment: CrossAxisAlignment.start,
children: [
Container(
width: 52,
height: 52,
decoration: BoxDecoration(
color: const Color(0xFFEDE7F6),
borderRadius: BorderRadius.circular(14),
),
child: Icon(
product.icon,
size: 28,
color: const Color(0xFF5E35B1),
),
),
const SizedBox(width: 16),
Expanded(
child: Column(
crossAxisAlignment: CrossAxisAlignment.start,
children: [
Container(
padding: const EdgeInsets.symmetric(
horizontal: 10,
vertical: 4,
),
decoration: BoxDecoration(
color: const Color(0xFF5E35B1),
borderRadius: BorderRadius.circular(20),
),
child: Text(
product.category,
style: const TextStyle(
color: Colors.white,
fontSize: 12,
fontWeight: FontWeight.w600,
),
),
),
const SizedBox(height: 8),
Text(
product.name,
style: const TextStyle(
fontSize: 17,
fontWeight: FontWeight.bold,
),
),
const SizedBox(height: 4),
Text(product.description),
],
),
),
const SizedBox(width: 12),
Text(
product.price,
style: const TextStyle(
fontWeight: FontWeight.bold,
color: Color(0xFF5E35B1),
),
),
],
),
),
),
),
),"""
required_blocks = [
old_constructor,
old_fields,
old_builder,
]
for block in required_blocks:
if block not in text:
raise SystemExit("A required Episode 16 code block was not found.")
text = text.replace(old_constructor, new_constructor, 1)
text = text.replace(old_fields, new_fields, 1)
for old, new in product_updates.items():
if old not in text:
raise SystemExit(f"Product entry was not found:\n{old}")
text = text.replace(old, new, 1)
text = text.replace(old_builder, new_builder, 1)
file_path.write_text(text)
print("Categories and redesigned product cards added successfully.")
PY
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The terminal confirms that category data and the redesigned product cards were added successfully.After the patch finishes, I open `main.dart` in Visual Studio Code.
The `Product` model now includes a category field, and every item in the product list has its own label.
The product data is becoming more descriptive without returning to repeated card code.
required this.category,Press enter or click to view image in full size

Visual Studio Code shows the new category field and the category assigned to every product.Press enter or click to view image in full size

The reusable card builder now displays a category label, a styled icon, and clearer product information.dart format lib/main.dart
flutter analyzePress enter or click to view image in full size

Dart formats the updated layout, and Flutter confirms that the new category system has no code issues.The code is clean, so I run the app in Chrome to see whether the new category system appears correctly on the screen.
I return to the terminal and start the Flutter web app:
flutter run -d chromeFlutter builds the project and opens the catalog in the browser.
This time, the change is immediately visible.
Each product card now has a category label above the product name.
Wireless Headphones is marked as Audio.
Smart Watch is marked as Wearables.
Digital Camera is marked as Photography.
The icons also sit inside their own styled containers, while the prices stand out more clearly on the right side.
The catalog still uses the same product list from Episode 16, but the interface now communicates much more information.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The redesigned catalog appears in Chrome with category labels, styled icons, clearer spacing, and stronger price text.I check each card to make sure Flutter is displaying the correct category from the product list.
Wireless Headphones shows Audio.
Smart Watch shows Wearables.
Digital Camera shows Photography.
This confirms that the category labels are not fixed text copied into the layout. Each label comes directly from its own product data.
The reusable card builder is doing the work, while the product list remains the single source of truth.
The interface looks more detailed, but the code behind it is still organized.
Episode 17 began with three products using the same simple card layout.
I extended the Product model with a category field, assigned a different category to every product, and updated the reusable card builder.
The catalog now displays more useful information without returning to repeated widget code.
I also improved the icons, spacing, card shape, and price styling.
The shelves were already organized.
Now they finally have labels.
In Episode 18, I will turn those category labels into an interactive filter.
Instead of only reading Audio, Wearables, and Photography, the user will be able to select a category and control which products appear on the screen.
